---
title: "Analysis Report"
output: html_document
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

# Background And Research Question

The UCL COVID-19 survey followed participants across three pandemic
waves. Wave 1 took place in May 2020, Wave 2 in September/October 2020,
and Wave 3 in February/March 2021 during the third UK lockdown.

This report examined whether depressive symptoms changed across the
three waves, and whether loneliness and perceived social support were
associated with depressive symptoms over time.

# Hypotheses

H1: Depressive symptoms will differ across the timepoints, with higher depressive
symptoms expected at Wave 3 compared with Wave 1.

H2: Greater loneliness scores will be associated with higher depressive symptoms
across waves.

H3: Lower perceived social support will be associated with higher
depressive symptoms across waves.

H4: Social support may moderate the loneliness-depression association,
such that the association between loneliness and depressive symptoms is
weaker among people reporting stronger social support.

# Analysis Strategy

A multilevel model was used, because the original dataset contains repeated
observations from the same participants across waves.

The final multilevel model was specified as:

`depression_score ~ wave + loneliness_c * social_support_c + sex + cohort + (1 | participant_id)`

In this equation, `(1 | participant_id)` represents a random intercept
for each participant. The interaction term
`loneliness_c * social_support_c` includes the main effects of
loneliness and social support, as well as their interaction.

The final modelling dataset included 26,437 participant-wave
observations from 13,371 participants. This was smaller than the full
cleaned dataset because rows with missing values in any model variable
were excluded from the multilevel models.

# Null Model And ICC

The null model estimated the extent to which depressive symptoms
clustered within participants. The between-participant variance was
1.512 and the residual variance was 0.996.

The intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) was 0.603, indicating that
approximately 60.3% of the variance in depressive symptoms was
attributable to differences between participants. This supported the use
of a mixed-effects model.

# Model Comparison

Models were built incrementally. Model 1 included wave, sex, and cohort.
Model 2 added loneliness and social support. Model 3 added the
interaction between loneliness and social support.

Model comparison showed that each step improved model fit. Adding wave,
sex, and cohort improved fit compared with the null model, χ²(5) =
1702.48, p \< .001. Adding loneliness and social support further
improved fit, χ²(2) = 7551.91, p \< .001. Adding the loneliness by
social support interaction also improved fit, χ²(1) = 34.49, p \< .001.
Model 3 was therefore retained as the final hypothesis-testing model.

# Final Model Results

Depressive symptoms differed significantly across waves. Compared with
Wave 1, depressive symptoms were significantly lower at Wave 2, b =
-0.092, p \< .001, and significantly higher at Wave 3, b = 0.041, p =
.013. This supports H1, although the increase from Wave 1 to Wave 3 was
small.

Loneliness was positively associated with depressive symptoms, b =
0.318, p \< .001. This supports H2.

Social support was negatively associated with depressive symptoms, b =
-0.131, p \< .001. This supports H3, indicating that higher perceived
social support was associated with lower depressive symptoms.

The loneliness by social support interaction was significant, b =
-0.015, p \< .001. This supports H4, suggesting that social support
slightly weakened the association between loneliness and depressive
symptoms.

# Wave Comparisons

Estimated marginal means showed that depressive symptoms were lowest at
Wave 2 and highest at Wave 3.

Wave 1 estimated mean = 1.31
Wave 2 estimated mean = 1.22
Wave 3 estimated mean = 1.35

Pairwise comparisons indicated that Wave 3 scores were significantly
higher than Wave 1 scores, supporting the prediction that depressive
symptoms would be higher during the third UK lockdown.

# Moderation Interpretation

The moderation check showed that the association between loneliness and
depressive symptoms was strongest at lower social support and weakest at
higher social support.

Low social support: b = 0.335
Average social support: b = 0.318
High social support: b = 0.301

This indicates that loneliness remained positively associated with
depressive symptoms at all levels of social support, but the association
was slightly weaker among participants reporting stronger social
support.

# Figures

## Estimated Depressive Symptoms Across Waves

```{r wave-figure, out.width="85%", fig.align="center"}
knitr::include_graphics("../figures/model_estimated_depression_by_wave.png")
```

## Loneliness And Depressive Symptoms By Social Support

```{r moderation-figure, out.width="85%", fig.align="center"}
knitr::include_graphics("../figures/loneliness_social_support_interaction.png")
```

# Conclusion

All hypotheses were supported by the findings. Depressive symptoms changed
across waves and were significantly higher at Wave 3 than Wave 1. Higher
loneliness was associated with higher depressive symptoms, while higher
social support was associated with lower depressive symptoms. Social
support also moderated the loneliness-depression association, although
the moderation effect was small.
